sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A community health study examined 120 adults enrolled in a smoking-cessation program. For each participant, researchers calculated social-network centrality within the support group on a 0–10 scale and recorded the number of smoke-free days during the first month. Participants were divided into low, moderate, and high centrality groups. Mean smoke-free days were 11, 18, and 24, respectively. Mean attendance at group meetings was 3.1, 4.7, and 6.0 sessions. Interviews suggested that highly connected participants received more encouragement and practical tips about coping with cravings between meetings.
questions.stem: Which group had the most smoke-free days during the first month?

choices.D: All groups had similar smoke-free days

explanations.A: Correct. Figure 1 shows 24 mean smoke-free days for the high-centrality group, higher than the moderate group at 18 and the low-centrality group at 11.
explanations.B: The low-centrality group had the fewest smoke-free days.
explanations.C: The moderate-centrality group improved relative to low centrality but did not match the high-centrality group.
explanations.D: The values differ markedly, so the groups are not similar.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Smoke-free days and support-group meeting attendance by network centrality.

questions.stem: The interviews most directly suggest that network ties provided what resource?

explanations.C: Correct. Encouragement and practical tips are classic examples of support flowing through social ties. They provide both emotional reinforcement and useful information for handling cravings, which can improve health behavior.
explanations.A: Actor-observer bias is a pattern in causal explanation, not a network resource.
explanations.B: Sensory memory is a cognitive construct unrelated to group ties.
explanations.D: The interviews highlight help and encouragement, not punishment or sanctions.

questions.stem: Which pattern in Figure 1 is most consistent with the idea that engagement and outcomes were linked?

choices.B: Groups with greater centrality attended more meetings and also had more smoke-free days
choices.A: Meeting attendance decreased as smoke-free days increased
choices.C: Centrality affected attendance but not smoke-free days
choices.D: Smoke-free days were highest in the least-connected group

explanations.B: Correct. Across the three groups, both attendance and smoke-free days rise together. That co-movement is consistent with the idea that being more embedded in the support network is associated with greater engagement and better cessation outcomes.
explanations.A: The figure shows both variables increasing together, not moving in opposite directions.
explanations.C: Both attendance and smoke-free days vary across centrality groups.
explanations.D: The least-connected group had the lowest smoke-free days.

questions.stem: A cessation counselor trying to apply these findings should most likely do what?

choices.D: Help isolated participants build more connections within the support group
choices.A: Reduce interaction so participants rely only on willpower
choices.B: Cancel meetings and replace them with a pamphlet
choices.C: Encourage participants to avoid discussing cravings with peers

explanations.D: Correct. If more connected participants had better attendance and more smoke-free days, then helping isolated members form ties could strengthen access to encouragement and practical coping advice. That recommendation follows directly from the pattern described in the passage.
explanations.A: Reducing interaction would cut off the very social resources associated with better outcomes.
explanations.B: A pamphlet cannot substitute for the network-based support highlighted here.
explanations.C: The interviews suggest that discussing cravings with peers was useful rather than harmful.

questions.stem: Why should the relationship between centrality and smoke-free days be interpreted cautiously?

choices.B: People who were already more motivated to quit may have become more central in the group
choices.A: Smoke-free days were counted over one month instead of one week
choices.C: The program included more than 100 adults

explanations.B: Correct. Because centrality was observed rather than randomly assigned, it may partly reflect underlying motivation, confidence, or social skill. Those same traits could help people quit, making the association potentially bidirectional or confounded.
explanations.A: The length of follow-up does not explain away the association.
explanations.C: A sample above 100 can still be observational and confounded.
explanations.D: Measuring attendance adds information but does not solve selection issues.

questions.passage: A school district surveyed 420 ninth graders about family routines. Students reported how many evenings per week they ate dinner with a parent or guardian, and they completed a questionnaire about past-month alcohol or cannabis use. Researchers grouped students into 0–1 family dinners, 2–4 dinners, or 5–7 dinners per week. Substance-use rates were 29%, 18%, and 11%, respectively. Mean parental-monitoring scores on a 1–10 scale were 4.8, 6.3, and 7.5. Students in the most frequent family-dinner group also reported the highest likelihood that a parent knew their close friends.
questions.stem: What relationship is shown in Figure 2 as weekly family dinners become more frequent?

choices.A: Substance use decreases while parental monitoring increases
choices.B: Both substance use and parental monitoring increase
choices.C: Substance use increases while parental monitoring decreases

explanations.A: Correct. From 0–1 dinners to 5–7 dinners, substance use falls from 29% to 11%, whereas parental monitoring rises from 4.8 to 7.5. The figure therefore shows opposite movement in the two variables.
explanations.B: The trend is not upward for both measures.
explanations.C: This response reverses the observed pattern.
explanations.D: The figure shows substantial change across groups.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Past-month substance use and parental monitoring by weekly family-dinner frequency.

questions.stem: Knowing a parent is aware of one’s close friends most directly reflects which concept?

choices.C: Informal social control within the family

explanations.C: Correct. Parents knowing their children’s friends reflects monitoring and the informal regulation of behavior through close relationships. That is a classic example of informal social control operating within a family context.
explanations.A: The placebo effect is a change produced by expectation in treatment contexts.
explanations.B: Functional fixedness concerns problem solving with objects.
explanations.D: Drive-reduction theory addresses motivation based on physiological needs.

questions.stem: Which explanation is most consistent with the pattern in the figure?

choices.D: Frequent family interaction may create more opportunities for supervision and communication
choices.A: Family meals directly change adolescent genes
choices.B: Substance use falls because eating dinner eliminates all peer influence
choices.C: Parental monitoring is unrelated to family routines

explanations.D: Correct. The passage reports both more frequent family dinners and higher monitoring among the lowest-use group. A reasonable interpretation is that shared routines increase supervision, communication, and awareness of adolescents’ activities.
explanations.A: Genes are not altered by dinner frequency in the way this choice suggests.
explanations.B: Peer influence is not eliminated by dinner; at most it may be buffered or monitored.
explanations.C: The figure shows a clear relationship between dinner frequency and monitoring.

questions.stem: A prevention program modeled on these data would most likely encourage which step?

choices.A: Creating regular family check-in routines that increase supervision and communication
choices.C: Telling parents that supervision does not matter after ninth grade
choices.D: Discouraging parents from asking about friends

explanations.A: Correct. The strongest pattern in the data combines frequent shared routines with higher monitoring and lower substance use. A prevention program would therefore try to strengthen regular parent-teen contact and communication rather than reduce it.
explanations.B: There is no evidence that removing activities is necessary or helpful.
explanations.C: The passage suggests that supervision remains relevant, not irrelevant, during ninth grade.
explanations.D: Discouraging questions about friends would undermine the very process associated with lower substance use.

questions.stem: What is the main inferential limitation of the study?

choices.C: Families were not randomly assigned to dinner frequency, so other family characteristics may account for part of the difference
choices.A: The study included only ninth graders
choices.B: Substance use was reported as percentages

explanations.C: Correct. This is a correlational survey. Families who eat together more often may also differ in structure, stress, work schedules, or parenting style, so dinner frequency cannot be identified as the sole cause of lower substance use.
explanations.A: Restricting the sample to ninth graders affects generalizability more than causal inference.
explanations.B: Using percentages is not the central interpretive problem.
explanations.D: The scale format for monitoring does not explain why the data are correlational.
